title: Body scan for sleep
subtitle: A ten-minute body scan, framed as a wind-down rather than a sleep technique.
excerpt: A guided body scan from the soles of the feet to the top of the head. Used in bed as the last ten minutes before sleep. Falling asleep mid-scan is allowed and common.
sourceNotes: Body scan meditation: public-domain lineage, secular MBSR adaptation by Jon Kabat-Zinn at UMass, late 1970s. Bedtime framing contributed by Rebecca Page, from Day 3 of SLEEP: A 30-Day Tapping Intensive (2025, ISBN 9798269649795).

practice.whenToUse: Use in bed as the last ten minutes before sleep on a wired night.
practice.whenNotToUse: If body-attention practices set off dissociation for you, use a feet-on-floor grounding instead. The body scan asks attention to move through every part of the body, which can be too much in a hard week.

content.text: A ten-minute guided body scan, used in bed as the last ten minutes before sleep. Attention moves slowly from the soles of the feet to the top of the head, one region at a time. Most people fall asleep mid-scan; that's the intended use.
New to body-based meditation? Read
body-based meditation
first.
The practice
Setting up
Lie down in bed, on your back. A pillow under the knees if your lower back is tender. Arms by your sides, palms up. Eyes closed.
Three slow breaths. Inhale through the nose, exhale through the mouth.
The scan
Bring your attention to the soles of your feet. Notice what's there — warmth, coolness, pressure against the sheet. Don't try to change anything. Two breaths.
Move up through the body, two breaths at each region:
the tops of the feet, the ankles
the calves, behind the knees
the thighs
the hips, the lower back
the belly, rising and falling with the breath
the chest, rising and falling with the breath
the fingertips, the hands, the wrists
the forearms, the elbows, the upper arms
the shoulders
the neck, the back of the head
the jaw, the cheeks, the small muscles around the eyes
the forehead, the top of the head
When the attention wanders, notice that it has wandered. Bring it back to the region you were on.
Coming back

If you've reached the top of the head and you're still awake, rest the attention on the whole body for a few breaths. Lengthen each exhale: in for four, out for six. Then four, out for seven. Then four, out for eight.
Where this practice comes from

Body scan meditation has multiple contemplative lineages. The form used here is the secular MBSR adaptation developed by Jon Kabat-Zinn at the University of Massachusetts in the late 1970s. The bedtime framing (body scan as sleep wind-down rather than as a daytime mindfulness sit) was contributed by Rebecca Page, from Day 3 of SLEEP: A 30-Day Tapping Intensive (2025).
